project_title_en: Advanced Analytics Triage of Visitor Record Applications
project_title_fr: Triage au moyen de l’analytique avancée des demandes de fiche du visiteur
description_en:
This project seeks to streamline the eligibility assessment for all visitor record applications in order to help IRCC decision-makers process applications more efficiently. The advanced data analytics system identifies routine applications for streamlined processing. When the system finds these applications, it can decide that an applicant is eligible, before sending the file to an officer to decide if the applicant remains admissible to Canada and make the final decision. To do this, it uses a mix of rules developed with IRCC officers and rules generated through machine learning based on previous IRCC case data. All rules are assessed for potential bias or discrimination.

For all other cases that are not considered routine, the tool helps to sort and assign cases to officers for manual review and decisions based on their level of complexity, using rules and criteria developed with IRCC officers. This means that these cases will be processed using the same process that was used before IRCC began using this new system. It will also provide case annotations, or notes for officers, which summarize basic application information for the officer to support faster processing. Officers still perform a full individual assessment of each application and its supporting documents.

The tool never refuses or recommends refusing applications. IRCC officers make the final decision to approve or refuse on all applications.

The tool is expected to result in faster processing times for all applications. 

Visitor records are one of IRCC’s most straightforward services. These applications are considered low impact, as clients are not applying for refugee status, permanent residence or citizenship, and are not entitled to study or work in Canada.
description_fr:
Ce projet vise à simplifier l’évaluation de la recevabilité pour toutes les demandes de fiche de visiteurs afin d’aider les décideurs d’IRCC à traiter les demandes plus efficacement. Le système avancé d’analyse de données identifie les demandes de routine pour un traitement rationalisé. Lorsque le système trouve ces demandes, il peut décider qu’un demandeur est recevable, avant d’envoyer le dossier à un agent pour décider si le demandeur est interdit de territoire et prendre la décision finale. Pour ce faire, il utilise un mélange de règles élaborées avec les agents d’IRCC et de règles générées par l’apprentissage automatique basé sur des données de cas antérieurs d’IRCC. Toutes les règles sont évaluées pour détecter tout biais ou discrimination potentiels. 

Pour tous les autres cas qui ne sont pas considérés comme routiniers, l’outil aide à trier et à assigner les demandes aux agents pour un examen manuel et les décisions en fonction de leur niveau de complexité, en utilisant des règles et des critères élaborés avec les agents d’IRCC. Cela signifie que ces demandes seront traitées selon le même processus qu’avant qu’IRCC ne commence à utiliser ce nouveau système. Il fournira également des annotations de cas, ou des notes à l’intention des agents, qui résument certains renseignements de base contenus dans la demande pour l’agent afin d’accélérer le traitement. Les agents procèdent toujours à une évaluation individuelle complète de chaque demande et de ses pièces justificatives.  

L’outil ne refuse ni ne recommande jamais de refuser des demandes. Les agents d’IRCC prennent la décision finale d’approuver ou de refuser toutes les demandes.  

L’outil devrait entraîner des temps de traitement plus rapides pour toutes les demandes.   

Les fiches des visiteurs sont l’un des services les plus simples d’IRCC. Ces demandes sont considérées comme ayant un faible impact, car les clients ne demandent pas le statut de réfugié, la résidence permanente ou la citoyenneté, et n’ont pas le droit d’étudier ou de travailler au Canada.

A negative decision may impact a client's travel plans or ability to remain in Canada. This impact, however, is likely to be temporary as clients whose application is refused can re-apply. IRCC approves many clients with prior refusals. The impact of most refusals is not perpetual. However, it should be noted that although the system will automate the approval of the eligibility of some clients, it will not make nor recommend any decisions to refuse. Officers will continue to make all decisions related to admissibility and all final decisions to approve or refuse.", "impact9": "item1-1", "impact10": "The system assesses data elements in incoming applications in order to triage (sort) applications based on complexity and automate positive eligibility determinations for applications with low complexity. The system is expected to have a low impact on the rights and freedoms of individuals, as the sorting of applications and facilitating approvals do not deny the rights of any clients. 

To ensure fairness and transparency, the system’s rules are based only on data elements with a clear link to legislative and regulatory requirements, and the system never refuses applications, nor does it recommend refusals. All refusals continue to be done by officers as per the current practice. All applications where eligibility cannot be approved automatically by the system will receive a full assessment by officers in accordance with standard practice. For applications where the positive eligibility determination is automated by the system, the system determines only that an applicant is eligible, before the application is sent to an officer to screen for admissibility. Thus, even in cases where the system approves the eligibility, officers continue to make all final decisions. 

Measures are also in place to mitigate against the potential risk that the triage function could influence officer decision-making. There is deliberate separation of officers from the system: officers are not aware of the rules used by the system, nor do they receive information about the analysis performed by the system. This separation mitigates the risk that officers could be unduly influenced by the system’s outputs (also known as “automation bias”). Additionally, an ongoing quality assurance process has been implemented to monitor whether officers make the same positive eligibility determinations as the system. This process ensures that biases have not been introduced by the system.

As a further safeguard, system rules go through an extensive review process. Before they are introduced, and at regular intervals afterward, rules are reviewed by experienced officers, legal, policy, and data science experts, as well as senior executives to ensure they are logical, understandable, non-discriminatory and aligned with established eligibility criteria. Regular monitoring and quality assurance measures also help make sure the system performs as intended and that any unforeseen negative impacts such as bias or discrimination can be identified early and mitigated.", "impact11": "item1-1", "impact12": "The system is expected to have little to no negative impact on the health and well-being of individuals as it will be used to triage applications and to automate certain positive eligibility determinations.", "impact13": "item1-1", "impact14": "The system is expected to have little to no negative impact on the economic interests of individuals as it will be used to triage applications and to automate certain positive eligibility determinations.", "impact15": "item1-1", "impact16": "The system is expected to have little to no negative impact on the environment as it will be used to triage applications and to automate certain positive eligibility determinations. In cases where eligibility is auto-approved, officers continue to make the admissibility determination and the final decision on each application. If an officer encounters information in the course of their admissibility assessment that may affect the positive eligibility determination of an application, they may revisit the eligibility determination. 

For applications that do not have the eligibility portion automatically approved by the system, both the eligibility and admissibility portions are done manually by officers.
